name: Pittidae
description: Pittas are colorful, ground-dwelling passerines with stout legs and short tails. They are found primarily in tropical Asia and Australasia, with one species in Africa.

children.name: Pitta
children.description: Pitta is the largest genus of pittas, containing most of the family's species across tropical Asia, Australasia, and Africa.

children.name: Pitta brachyura
children.description: The Indian pitta is a brightly colored bird with nine distinct plumage colors, found in the Indian subcontinent. It is migratory, wintering in Sri Lanka and southern India.

children.name: Pitta moluccensis
children.description: The blue-winged pitta is a migratory species breeding in Southeast Asia and wintering in the Malay Peninsula and Indonesia. It has a bold black head stripe and brilliant blue wing patches.

children.name: Pitta sordida
children.description: The hooded pitta is a common and widespread species with a black head and bright green body. It inhabits lowland forests and plantations across much of Southeast Asia.

children.name: Pitta gurneyi
children.description: Gurney's pitta is a critically endangered species restricted to southern Myanmar and adjacent Thailand. Males have a striking yellow belly and blue crown.

children.name: Pitta superba
children.description: The superb pitta is endemic to the islands of Manus and nearby islets in Papua New Guinea. It has iridescent blue-black plumage with a bright red belly.

children.name: Pitta iris
children.description: The rainbow pitta is endemic to northern Australia, where it inhabits monsoon forests and mangroves. Its plumage includes green, blue, red, black, and buff markings.

children.name: Pitta versicolor
children.description: The noisy pitta is found in eastern Australia and New Guinea, named for its loud, distinctive call. It has a black head, white throat, and green upperparts with blue wing flashes.

children.name: Pitta megarhyncha
children.description: A colourful pitta restricted to mangrove forests of coastal Bangladesh, Myanmar, Thailand, Malaysia, and Indonesia. It has a black head, white throat, green back, and buff underparts with a bright red belly. It is one of the few pittas specialised for mangrove habitat.

children.name: Pitta baudii
children.description: A stunning pitta endemic to Borneo, with a brilliant blue head and nape, black face, white throat, and deep blue upperparts with a red belly. It inhabits lowland rainforests and is increasingly threatened by habitat loss.

children.name: Pitta nympha
children.description: A migratory pitta that breeds in East Asia (Japan, Korea, Taiwan, and eastern China) and winters in Southeast Asia. It has a black head with a buff eyebrow, green upperparts, and pinkish-buff underparts with a bright red belly. It is considered vulnerable due to habitat loss on its breeding grounds.

children.name: Pitta vigorsii
children.description: A recently described pitta endemic to Siau Island in Indonesia, with a black head, white throat, green upperparts, and rich buff underparts. It is critically endangered, with a tiny population restricted to a single small island.

children.name: Pitta maxima
children.description: A large pitta endemic to the Moluccas in Indonesia, with a black head, white throat and breast, bright green upperparts, and a black belly. It inhabits lowland and montane forests on Halmahera and neighbouring islands. Its simple, whistled song is a characteristic sound of Moluccan forests.

children.name: Pitta concinna
children.description: A colourful pitta endemic to the Lesser Sunda Islands of Indonesia, with a black head, white throat, green upperparts, and rufous underparts. It inhabits monsoon forests and is locally common on Flores, Sumbawa, and neighbouring islands.

children.name: Pitta elegans
children.description: A medium-sized pitta found across the Lesser Sunda Islands and Moluccas, with a black head, white throat, green upperparts, and pale buff underparts with a red vent. Its plumage varies geographically across its numerous island subspecies.

children.name: Pitta steerii
children.description: A striking pitta endemic to the Philippines, with a black head, bright azure-blue upperparts, and white underparts with a red belly. It inhabits lowland forests on Mindanao, Leyte, and Samar. It is threatened by ongoing deforestation across its range.

children.name: Hydrornis
children.description: A genus of medium-sized to large pittas found in tropical Asia, characterised by more subdued plumage than the typical pittas, often with intricate barred or banded patterns. They are secretive, ground-dwelling birds of forest understorey.

children.name: Hydrornis soror
children.description: A medium-sized pitta with a blue rump, brown upperparts, and finely barred underparts, found in lowland and montane forests of Southeast Asia from Thailand to Vietnam and Hainan. It is a shy, ground-dwelling species that forages in the leaf litter.

children.name: Hydrornis oatesi
children.description: A large, handsome pitta with a chestnut nape, blue upperparts, and pale underparts with black barring, found in montane forests of mainland Southeast Asia from Myanmar to Vietnam. It is a secretive ground-dweller, more often heard than seen, with a loud, whistled song.

children.name: Hydrornis cyaneus
children.description: A stunning pitta with brilliant blue upperparts, a black face mask, and pale underparts with blue barring on the breast, found in lowland and montane forests of mainland Southeast Asia. It forages on the forest floor, turning over leaves in search of insects.

children.name: Hydrornis irena
children.description: A brilliantly coloured pitta with a yellow crown, black eye-stripe, blue upperparts, and heavily barred black-and-white underparts, found in lowland forests of the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo. It is a secretive ground-dweller with a loud, distinctive whistle.

children.name: Hydrornis guajanus
children.description: A striking pitta endemic to Java and Bali, with a yellow crown, black eye-stripe, blue upperparts, and densely barred underparts. It inhabits lowland and montane forests, foraging on the forest floor for insects and other invertebrates.
